summary: Separate the agent/host from tool/resource servers using explicit protocol boundaries and schema-based messages.
risks: Remote servers can become uncontrolled capability surfaces
Tool descriptions may mislead agents if not audited
Protocol adoption can create dependency creep
rules_for_imperium: Do not install MCP as a host requirement for New Generation V1.
Represent every local skill as a skill bundle with manifest, input schema, output schema, side effects, receipts, and owner/custodes admission.
Separate tool discovery from tool execution.
Never let tool descriptions override Doctrinarium/Custodes policy.

summary: Persist state at every step so human review, resume, memory, time travel, and fault recovery are possible.
risks: Leaking sensitive context into long-term state
rules_for_imperium: Every route step writes a checkpoint card.
Every checkpoint has run_id, route_id, agent_id, input_refs, output_refs, verdict, and resume_policy.
Human/Owner approval checkpoints are first-class route nodes.
Checkpoint data goes to RUNS layer unless promoted through gates.

summary: Define specialized agents in human-readable files with frontmatter/config describing identity, scope, tools, memory, and appearance.
rules_for_imperium: Every Organ-Agent gets AGENT_MANIFEST.json plus ROLE_CONTRACT.md.
Tool access is allowlisted, not inherited by default.
Memory scope is explicit: none, run-only, agent-owned, permanent, correction-only.
Visual CLI identity is documented but never replaces machine status.

summary: Start/stop hooks expose agent lifecycle events, transcript paths, and context injection points.
rules_for_imperium: All lifecycle injections must be visible in run receipts.
AgentStart and AgentStop events go to event ledger.
No hidden context injection; added context must be declared.
Transcript and outbox paths are recorded as evidence refs.

summary: The agent performs better when the computer interface is intentionally designed: commands, tools, environment, parser, and history handling are shaped for the task.
rules_for_imperium: Local Organ-Agents expose narrow CLI commands, not arbitrary shell by default.
Every action has an action_card with expected inputs/outputs/side_effects.
History/context is summarized by explicit processors, not silently truncated.
Command chunking and report budget are enforced.

summary: Agent work should produce inspectable trajectories, not only final answers.
rules_for_imperium: Route sessions write JSONL event traces.
Owner-readable trace summaries are generated separately from machine traces.
Sanctum reads trace indexes, not full flood logs.
Trace retention and compression policy required.

summary: Coding agents need a controlled workspace to plan, edit, execute commands, test, and produce end-to-end changes.
rules_for_imperium: Organ-Agents do not directly mutate canon; Servitor performs controlled implementation.
Execution sandboxes must have allowed paths, deny paths, cleanup, receipts, and rollback plan.
Parallelism requires Custodes concurrency admission.
Large-repo dependency mapping should become an Administratum/Mechanicus capability.

summary: Complex output can be created by role-specialized agents following standardized operating procedures and passing structured artifacts downstream.
rules_for_imperium: Every organ role needs concrete input/output schemas and stop conditions.
Downstream agents must validate upstream artifacts, not blindly accept them.
SOP outputs must be tied to receipts and gates.
Avoid free-form roleplay; use Organ contracts.

summary: Separate structured flow control from collaborative agent teams: flows manage state/branches, crews solve delegated tasks.
rules_for_imperium: Sanctum/route manager controls flow, not free agent chat.
Organ groups are invoked only through route cards.
Memory extraction requires provenance and learning policy.
Guardrails and observability are mandatory.

summary: Agents communicate through asynchronous messages and modular typed components, with tracing/debugging and cross-language extensibility.
risks: Hard debugging if typed receipts are weak
rules_for_imperium: All agent messages use typed envelopes.
Async messaging allowed only with route/session IDs and ordering rules.
Cross-language adapters must declare runtime, dependencies, and receipts.
Observability is part of the protocol, not optional.

summary: Agents can be wrapped as tools, but side-effectful/internal-state tools must not run in unsafe parallel.
rules_for_imperium: Every Organ-Agent callable as a tool must declare side_effects and concurrency_policy.
Disable parallel calls for stateful agents unless explicitly admitted.
Set max_tool_iterations / max_route_steps budgets.
Structured outputs require schema validation.

source_systems: Claude Code subagent color identity as weak reference
summary: CLI interactions should be readable, colored, panel-based, and status-first, without hiding evidence or faking success.
rules_for_imperium: Default CLI output uses readable sections, icons/symbols only if encoding-safe, color bands, status badges, and concise tables.
No green for unverified status.
Machine outputs are JSON/JSONL; visual CLI is a presentation layer.
Start with no external dependency or optional Rich/Textual adapter behind a verified capability gate.
